Automating Batch Consolidation with Bin-Packing in R

Project
R
Optimisation
An inventory controller spent ~30 minutes a week hand-packing stock into batches. I replaced it with an R script that solves the packing problem optimally — dropping the job to a few seconds.
Author

Tran Dang Huy (Andy)

Published

December 1, 2025

This is a public write-up of a work project, so the real stock data has been kept private. The numbers in the worked example below are made-up illustrations, not real figures.

The problem: a weekly packing puzzle done by hand

An inventory controller on the Logistics team had a recurring task. Every week she received a list of stock — many rows of items, each with a quantity — and had to consolidate them into batches. A batch normally holds up to 36 items (a handful of special items, kept in a separate list, allow 72).

The catch is that you can’t just throw anything together. A batch can only combine rows that share the same item type and come from the same client and establishment (dispatch location). And the goal isn’t just to make batches — it’s to make good batches: fill each one as close to full as possible so you’re left with as few small, half-empty batches as possible. Big quantities like 26 or 15 should be consolidated into full batches; little stragglers like 1 or 2 should be what’s left over, not what fills a batch.

Doing this well, by hand, took about 30 minutes every week — and it was easy to end up with more loose batches than necessary.

What I built

A single R script. She drops the week’s stock export into a Database folder, runs the script, and gets back an Excel file where every row is tagged with a batch number (Group) — packed optimally. That’s it. The 30-minute puzzle became a few seconds.

Under the hood, this is a classic bin-packing problem, and I solved each batch’s fill with dynamic programming so the result is genuinely optimal, not just a rough guess.

How it works

Step 1 — Apply the business rules first

Before any packing, the script cleans and filters the data: it drops items that aren’t ready to ship (still pending a temperature change), applies a small cost-centre correction, and loads the special “list of 72” that gets the larger batch size.

data <- read_excel("Database/Data.xlsx", skip = 4) %>%
  filter(is.na(`Temperature Change Due`)) %>%      # only ship-ready stock
  mutate(`Cost Centre` = if_else(Client == "DVP", "822", `Cost Centre`))

list_72 <- read_excel("Database/List of 72.xlsx")   # items that pack up to 72

Step 2 — Only pack things that are allowed to go together

The script finds every unique combination of (Client, Establishment, Product) and treats each one as its own independent packing problem. That guarantees a batch never mixes item types or dispatch locations, and it also keeps each sub-problem small enough to solve exactly.

keys <- unique(df[, c("Client", "Estab", "Product")])
for (k in seq_len(nrow(keys))) {
  # ...pack just the rows matching this Client + Estab + Product...
  bs <- if (prod_val %in% list_72) 72 else 36   # special items get the bigger cap
}

Step 3 — Fill each batch as full as possible (the interesting bit)

Within a group, the script repeatedly:

  1. picks the largest remaining item as the “anchor” of a new batch, then
  2. finds the best combination of the other items that fits in the space left, getting as close to the cap as possible.

That second step is a subset-sum solved with dynamic programming — it checks which totals up to the remaining capacity are achievable, then reconstructs the exact items that hit the highest one:

best_subset_under <- function(qty, cap = 36) {
  ord  <- order(qty, decreasing = TRUE)
  qty2 <- qty[ord]
  dp <- rep(FALSE, cap + 1); dp[1] <- TRUE   # dp[s] = "can we exactly reach total s?"

  for (i in seq_along(qty2)) {
    qi <- qty2[i]
    if (is.na(qi) || qi <= 0 || qi > cap) next
    for (s in seq(cap, qi, by = -1)) {
      if (!dp[s + 1] && dp[(s - qi) + 1]) { dp[s + 1] <- TRUE; ... }
    }
  }
  target <- if (dp[cap + 1]) cap else max(which(dp) - 1, 0)  # best fill <= cap
  # ...walk back through the table to recover which items made up `target`...
}

Anchoring on the biggest item first means large quantities get placed into full batches early, and the small stragglers are what remain — exactly the behaviour that was wanted. Any single row bigger than the cap simply gets a batch to itself.

A worked example

Say one item type, at one location, has these quantities waiting to ship, into batches of 36:

26, 15, 12, 10, 8, 3, 1

The engine produces:

Batch Items packed Total
A 26 + 10 36 ✅ full
B 15 + 12 + 8 + 1 36 ✅ full
C 3 3 — small leftover

Two perfectly full batches and a single small remainder — instead of several awkward, half-filled ones.

Challenges and what I learned

  • Bin-packing is hard in general (NP-hard), so a brute-force search over everything wouldn’t scale. Splitting the data by the grouping rules first shrinks it into many small problems, each of which DP can solve exactly and instantly.
  • The greedy-only trap. Just stuffing the biggest items in first (a pure greedy approach) leaves gaps. Pairing a greedy anchor with an exact subset-sum fill gets the best of both: speed and full batches.
  • Edge cases matter in operations. Oversized rows, missing quantities, and type mismatches between the data and the “list of 72” all had to be handled, or a single odd row would break the run.

The outcome

A weekly 30-minute manual puzzle became a one-click, few-seconds job — and the batches it produces are more consistently full than hand-packing achieved. It’s a small script, but it’s a good example of turning a real operational headache into a clean, optimal, repeatable process.
